# Transcrição: 01 Pricing Structures

- **Fonte:** 01 Pricing Structures
- **Duração:** 25:52
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Hey guys, welcome to probably one of the most important trainings in the Academy and that is how to price yourself. So what we're going to cover in today's training is we're going to look at different pricing structures because there's quite a few different models that agencies can use to price and different models that we in fact encourage within the Academy. And we're going to look at the idea of own funnels, leased funnels, and then pricing in practice. What you need to do as part of today's training as well is really spend some time putting together your service document. There's a blank template as well as an example template available underneath this video for you. Just be aware that it can take about two to four hours to pull one of these together properly depending in which niche that you're operating in. And once you have this, however, once you have this in place, it's kind of godsend because your entire team knows exactly how much they can price, the sort of leverage that they can give in terms of discounting. And it means that you don't need to be as involved in that part of your business anymore and the company, the employees know how to protect your margins as account managers or business developers. Okay? So a funnel just to explain because we're going to be talking a lot about funnels in here. These are online assets. So you may have heard me use Facebook funnels, coaching funnels. In the situation of pricing, we're talking about the entire thing. So the email sequences, the retargeting, the Facebook campaigns, the landing pages, etc., etc. All right? Now, typically there's two types of funnels. There's what we call owned funnels and leased funnels. Now why is this important and why am I starting off with this? Because these two types of funnels will significantly impact the way you charge a client. So let me talk to you briefly about both of these and you'll be quite astounded by one of these models actually. So owned funnels is pretty much what probably 95% of agencies are doing at the moment. And that is where the client owns all of the assets. So what that means is the client owns the ad account. They own the emails. They own the Facebook campaigns. They own the landing pages. Think about it this way. If you and your client broke up, who would all of those assets go to? Would you own them or would the client? If it's the client, then that's an owned funnel, right? They paid you to build it. And so if the relationship splits or part ways, then you don't need to... They own that funnel, right? So there's a little bit more risk for you in this situation, especially if you're not

## [03:00 - 06:00]

charging or billing the client properly, meaning you're not charging upfront for this. And this is why with owned funnels, it's very important to charge upfront for them. On the flip side of that, there's what we call leased funnels. And these are really, really cool. Now leased funnels pretty much only work in the lead gen space. And you can kind of do it in the coaching space. So you can lease some coaching funnels. It's a little bit trickier to do. But this works really well for lead gen like realtors, mortgage lenders, insurance, et cetera, et cetera. And what this is is that you have a funnel and it generates leads. And it might generate leads at like $50 a pot. And what you're doing is you're basically leasing that funnel to a client. You're saying, hey, you want some leads? I've got some lawyer leads here. You just need to lease this from me. So you own the assets. You own the ad account. You own the campaigns. You own everything except for the actual service that's being delivered. This means that if the relationship splits up, the client basically doesn't walk away with anything. You even own the leads. So you can even sell those leads to another lawyer later on. So it's quite a cool model. And investors really like this model because with leased funnels, you can pretty much lock people into like a 12 month leasing contract. And you are stipulating inside of your contract that they're only allowed to use the funnel as long as they use your agency as the advertiser. So if they decide to hire another freelancer or another agency, they lose access to that funnel. So you're only leasing the funnel for like a dollar a month. You're not trying to make money off of that. But where you make your money is that they have to hire you as an agency. And that's why investors really like this model is because you're locking people into long term contracts with your agency, which really makes your books look good. So there's a little bit less risk for you involved here. It is a little bit less desirable to the client, but funnily enough, it's easier to pitch in because obviously you don't have to charge as much for this. You're not setting up a brand new funnel from scratch and trying to test and validate something. You're already working with something that works. And all you're doing is selling on those leads and giving them access to it. Okay, so here are the different pricing structures that are very common amongst agencies. So the first one, which is probably where most of the big agencies like the Satchis operate at is called the agency model. And that's where you are charging a percentage of ad spend. So if you're spending $1,000 in ad spend, they're going to take 15%. Obviously you would need to spend a lot more in ad spend. But this is the typical model.

## [06:00 - 09:02]

Now this model doesn't really work that well for clients nowadays because the incentive for the agency isn't necessarily to get you better results. It's to spend more money. The more money they spend, the more money they make. So it's not always the best model and it typically only really works when you're working with big brands or at the big agency level. The next pricing structure is performance based. And this is really something that I really got into. And I sort of pioneered this model about two years ago because I was realizing that the agency model was a problem with certain clients. And I was confident enough and I decided to put my money where my mouth was. Now this is where you're actually taking a cut of the results. So if you are doing $200,000 in revenue, you're going to take a 10% cut of their revenue. This model is very, very risky because if you do not audit the client properly, you can get yourself in a lot of trouble and you may stand to make no money. However, it is also extremely lucrative if you do it right. Because as the client scales, so do you. So if you're looking at the retainer model, which is like a set $5,000 fee per month, well that's going to work until they go and start spending $30,000, $50,000, $100,000 in ad spend per month. And all of a sudden you have to hire a full time person just to look after that account and you're not making any money anymore off your $5,000 retainer. So the performance based model works really well because it is scalable as the client scales and the incentive for the agency isn't necessarily to spend more money, it's to get better results with less money because everyone stands to make more money. Now the third pricing structure is the retainer model. And this is probably something you're really familiar with and that's simply where you're charging a set amount each month, it rolls over. When you get clients on this model, it's really important to put them on an automated recurring billing system and there's lots of systems out there. But essentially you can even do it through a Stripe subscription for example. You want to make sure that you're not having to send an invoice at the beginning of each month and then having to remind them a million times, you want an automated billing system that just takes the money out right away. Because it's a set amount, they've agreed to it and there should be no messing around or faffing around with invoices. Now what we also have is the bulk buy model. This is where a customer will purchase a bulk amount of leads. Again, this works mostly in the lead gen space. So you are selling them say a hundred leads at a certain price at $50 per lead.

## [09:02 - 12:02]

It's very similar to the lease, it works really well with the least funnel model. And then we have a hybrid version where you're taking a combination of these pricing structures and mashing them together. So for example, you could do performance based and instead of taking a 10% cut, you're taking a 5% cut but you're also going to charge a $5,000 a month base retainer. That's what would be known as a hybrid model. Now don't freak out too much about the numbers behind this. We're going to be putting together a service document together. And once you develop that, you're going to get a really clear sense of where your prices need to sit at. All right, but just so you understand, these are the different pricing structures. So if you look at the different kinds of funnels, so owned, leased, and hybrid funnels, some of the models or pricing models that work really well for owned funnels is the agency model, the performance based model, retainer, and hybrid. Pretty much most of them under the leased funnels, it's the same thing except what we also have here is the bulk by version as I mentioned. Now for a lot of the leased funnels, they're going to be paying at the end of the month. If it's a brand new relationship, I would recommend trying to get some of that amount upfront. Okay, just to mitigate some of your risk. And the reason they can pay at the end of the month is that you own the asset. You can sell that funnel to someone else. You can sell those leased to someone else. So the risk is mitigated. With hybrid funnels, usually it's the agency model and retainer or the performance based model with the retainer. They would normally pay the retainer, so the set up fee at the beginning of the month, and then they would pay the performance based or the ad spend percentage at the end of the month. Now, if you're doing a pure performance based model, such as what we're seeing in the owned funnels, what I would recommend here is that if it's a brand new working relationship, you get them to pay a predicted amount upfront. So let's say you're saying they're going to spend $50,000 in ad spend and you predict they're going to make 100K and your fees are going to be $10,000. You're going to get them to pay $3,000 upfront just to mitigate some of the risk. And you just explain to them it's a new working relationship. There's a lot of risk involved for us. We've been burnt in the past by clients that got greedy and we're just charging this to keep us safe. If we don't hit our marks, it rolls over to the next month. If it's not a new relationship, so let's say you've been working with that client for three months doing maybe on a retainer basis or a set up fee and you trust the client, they've been paying their invoice, it's absolutely fine to just go straight on performance based and charge at the end of the month. But you're the best one to judge that relationship. So just make sure you get that.

and I'm going to take you in there in a moment and show you how we're going to do that. And we're going to charge upfront for new relationships.
